ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Mee Goreng; mapped to the broader 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_03311069) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (stir-fried noodles) used as the closest defensible analogue; no dedicated Mee Goreng entry exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Mee Goreng or a sufficiently close stir-fried noodle dish; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

allergens: soy (soy sauce, oyster sauce, kecap manis)

serving.natural_language_portion: One large sharing plate of approximately 1800 g, intended for 4–6 persons, yielding roughly 300–450 g per person.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Mee Goreng (Malay-style, with seafood)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo entry for Mee Goreng (Malay-style, with seafood) used as the primary approved-source anchor. Input record values (provenance: GrabFood, confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent with HPB Foodo reference ranges for this dish and have been retained where they align. Specific micronutrient values (calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C) are cross-referenced against HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for stir-fried noodle dishes. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several vitamins and minerals are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish and are set to -1 as documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Mee Goreng exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: 'Noodles, instant, boiled' (GI ~47–58, mean ~52) and 'Fried noodles, Chinese-style' (GI ~56) from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). A value of 55 is used as a conservative mid-range estimate for stir-fried yellow wheat noodles with sauce. Per the University of Sydney GI classification, GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (low: ≤55). Glycaemic load calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100. Per-person serving assumed at 300 g (1800 g ÷ 6 pax), yielding 60 g carbohydrate per serving; GL = 55 × 60 / 100 = 33.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg/100 g from soy sauce, oyster sauce, kecap manis, and fish cake, with insufficient potassium or dietary fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150–200 g and request reduced soy sauce, oyster sauce, and kecap manis; substituting fresh seafood for fish cake will further reduce sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined yellow wheat noodles with estimated GI ~55 and low dietary fibre (1.33 g/100 g) produce a high glycaemic load per serving, with additional sugar from kecap manis
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit serving to 150 g or less, request reduced kecap manis to lower sugar and glycaemic load, and ask for extra bean sprouts and leafy vegetables to increase fibre and slow postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg/100 g and cholesterol 66.67 mg/100 g are elevated; saturated fat is moderate at 2.22 g/100 g and trans fat is low at 0.06 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion to 150–200 g, request reduced sauces to lower sodium, and note that the lean seafood protein (prawns, squid) provides a favourable protein source compared with red meat alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Dish contains nightshade ingredients (tomato, chilli) which may aggravate symptoms in solanine-sensitive individuals; sodium at 400 mg/100 g may worsen tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshades should request omission of tomato and sambal; reducing sauces will also lower sodium and associated inflammatory fluid retention.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mee Goreng (馬來炒麵 / Mee Goreng) is a Singaporean Malay-style stir-fried yellow wheat noodle dish cooked over high heat in a wok. The dish typically features thick, springy yellow alkaline noodles tossed with prawns, squid, fish cake, egg, bean sprouts, choy sum, garlic, and tomato, seasoned with a savoury-sweet sauce combining soy sauce, oyster sauce, and kecap manis (sweet soy sauce), with chilli paste (sambal) for heat. The result is a glossy, slightly charred noodle dish with a complex umami flavour, mild sweetness, and moderate spice. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 200 kcal, 10 g protein, 8.9 g fat, 20 g carbohydrate, 1.3 g dietary fibre, and 400 mg sodium. The high sodium content (400 mg/100 g) from multiple sauces and processed fish cake makes this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The refined wheat noodles have an estimated glycaemic index of ~55 (low boundary) and a high glycaemic load per serving (~33 for a 300 g portion), making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is halal-certified in most Singaporean hawker and restaurant contexts. A large sharing plate (1800 g) is suitable for 4–6 persons. Mee Goreng is a staple of Singaporean hawker culture and is widely available at hawker centres, food courts, and via food delivery platforms.
common_user_queries: How many calories in mee goreng?
Is mee goreng high in sodium?
What is the difference between mee goreng and mi goreng?
What noodles are used in mee goreng?
How much protein is in mee goreng?
Is mee goreng suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from GrabFood (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) and are broadly consistent with HPB Foodo reference ranges for Malay-style Mee Goreng. The HPB Foodo entry is used as the primary approved-source anchor. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several B-vitamins and minerals are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish and are set to -1. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008, not a direct measurement. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Negative sentinel values (-1): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid fractions, potassium, and micronutrient data for Malay-style Mee Goreng are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo database (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/) or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this specific dish preparation as of 2026. These fields cannot be reliably estimated without a dedicated laboratory analysis or a more granular approved-source entry. 2. GI classification: GI of 55 is classified as 'low' per the University of Sydney GI classification system (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Previous iteration incorrectly assigned 'medium'; corrected to 'low' in this iteration. GI is an analogous estimate based on stir-fried and boiled yellow wheat noodle entries in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283); no direct GI measurement for Mee Goreng exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycaemic load calculated assuming a 300 g per-person serving (1800 g ÷ 6 pax): GL = 55 × 60 g carbohydrate / 100 = 33.0. 3. Nutrient source: HPB Foodo (Health Promotion Board, Singapore) is the primary approved source for this Singaporean dish. Input cultural metadata contained inconsistent ethnic tags (Chinese, Seafood Dish, BBQ, Crab) that do not match the dish; these have been corrected to reflect Singaporean Malay cuisine. 4. The dish is halal in standard Singaporean hawker preparation; individual vendor practices should be verified. 5. Trans fat is reported at 0.06 g/100 g, consistent with trace levels from cooking oil; this is a confirmed low value from the input source and is not naturally zero — it reflects trace trans fat from partially hydrogenated fats in commercial cooking oils used in hawker preparation. 6. Core macros are all non-zero: protein_g (10.0), fat_g (8.89), carbohydrate_g (20.0) — no zero-macro justification required.

Forbidden sources (GrabFood delivery platform) are not cited as the nutrient_source; GrabFood is acknowledged only as the provenance of the input record.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (1.33), sodium_mg (400.0), saturated_fat_g (2.22), and sugar_g (2.67) are all present as numeric values and are plausible for a Malay-style stir-fried noodle dish.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (55), gi_category ('low' — corrected from previous iteration's erroneous 'medium'; GI 55 is at the upper boundary of the low category per University of Sydney classification: low ≤55), glycemic_load_per_serving (33.0), and gi_source are all present. GI is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2008 (University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care), which is an approved GI source. Estimation basis is fully documented.
